title: How Many Qubits Does a Quantum Computer Need? - Quantum Computing Report
gist: Useful-quantum thresholds are not one number: applications split into three bands (tens to hundreds, thousands, and millions of qubits), and resource estimates are collapsing faster through algorithmic improvement than through hardware progress, so diligence must interrogate the physical-to-logical ratio, modality, and algorithmic compression behind any vendor claim.

themes.theme: Physical-to-logical ratio as the key diligence variable

insights.claim: The ratio of physical qubits to one reliable logical qubit ranges from roughly 2:1 at the optimistic end to over 2,000:1 at the demanding end, depending on technology and target error rate, making headline raw-qubit counts a poor proxy for capability.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: When valuing a quantum hardware company, discount raw-qubit press releases and demand the logical-qubit translation; two firms both claiming 'thousands of qubits' can be orders of magnitude apart in real capability.
insights.quote: The ratio between the two, depending on the technology and the target error rate, can range from roughly 2 to 1 at the optimistic end to over 2,000 to 1 at the demanding end.
insights.locator: Not all quantum bits (or qubits) are created equal

insights.claim: The physical-to-logical ratio is one of the single most important variables for estimating when quantum computing becomes commercially relevant, because two systems claiming 'thousands of qubits' can be non-equivalent if one counts raw hardware and the other counts error-corrected logical qubits.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Use the logical-vs-raw distinction as the first filter on any commercialization-timeline claim before underwriting it.
insights.quote: The physical-to-logical ratio is, in many ways, one of the single most important variables for anyone estimating when quantum computing becomes commercially relevant.

themes.theme: Band One: near-reach scientific applications (tens to hundreds of logical qubits)

insights.claim: Carefully chosen chemistry and materials-science problems may become genuinely interesting with roughly 25 to 100 logical qubits, making early fault-tolerant scientific value achievable with relatively small but reliable machines.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: The first useful fault-tolerant machine does not need to be enormous, so watch reliability/error-rate milestones over raw qubit-count milestones for the earliest commercial inflection.
insights.quote: recent work suggests useful early fault-tolerant simulations may be possible with roughly 25 to 100 logical qubits
insights.locator: Band One: Early Scientific Applications Within Reach

insights.claim: Quantinuum reported in 2026 that it used 64 logical qubits to simulate quantum magnetism at a scale it described as exceedingly difficult for classical methods, using a low-overhead encoding on trapped ions.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: One of the few public examples of a vendor tying error-corrected logical qubits to a real many-body application; a marker that trapped-ion players are pulling ahead on logical-qubit demonstrations.
insights.quote: Quantinuum, a quantum computing company specialising in trapped-ion hardware, reported in 2026 that it had used 64 logical qubits to simulate quantum magnetism at a scale it described as exceedingly difficult for classical methods
insights.locator: Materials science and condensed matter physics

insights.claim: Quantum resource estimates for simulating X-ray spectra of degrading lithium-excess battery cathodes came in at roughly 100 logical qubits for one measurement and around 414 logical qubits for a more demanding type, both led by researchers at Xanadu and collaborators.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Commercially valuable battery-materials simulation sits inside the near-term band-one bracket, suggesting battery R&D could be among the first commercial quantum use cases.
insights.quote: The second tackled a more demanding type of X-ray measurement used to track how cathodes break down over time, and came in at around 414 logical qubits.

insights.claim: MOTH released the world's first consumer product powered by live quantum hardware in May 2026, an open-access game called Quantum Backrooms, marking an early quantum commercialization effort in gaming.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Gaming is an unexpected early consumer-facing quantum vertical; watch the quantum-software layer (not just hardware) for early commercialization signals.
insights.quote: MOTH officially released the world’s first consumer product powered by live quantum hardware in May 2026, an open-access game called Quantum Backrooms

themes.theme: Band Two: commercially ambitious applications (thousands of logical qubits)

insights.claim: The FeMoco nitrogen-fixation benchmark was placed at roughly 2,100 to 2,200 logical qubits and about four million physical qubits in a 2021 study, but a 2025 algorithmic improvement cut the estimate to roughly 1,500 logical qubits.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that algorithmic compression alone can shave ~30% off logical-qubit requirements in four years; underwrite roadmaps assuming algorithm gains, not just hardware scaling.
insights.quote: A major 2021 study placed FeMoco at around 2,100 to 2,200 logical qubits and roughly four million physical qubits under specific assumptions, and a 2025 algorithmic improvement reduced the estimate further to roughly 1,500 logical qubits.

insights.claim: Cytochrome P450 enzymes, which mediate roughly 75% of human drug metabolism, could have their largest model assessed with around 200 to 400 logical qubits at the algorithmic layer, translating to roughly 4.6 million physical qubits and 73 hours of runtime at a 0.1% physical error rate (Google, Boehringer Ingelheim collaboration in PNAS).
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Pharma metabolism modeling is a concrete band-two target with named big-pharma backing; track follow-up papers that compress the qubit/runtime requirement further.
insights.quote: Cytochrome P450 enzymes, which mediate roughly 75% of human drug metabolism, sit in similar territory.

insights.claim: The prediction market Kalshi, as of May 2026, implied a 36% probability before 2030 and 52% before 2035 of a quantum-advantage benchmark being met, defined as simulating FeMoco and CYP450 or cracking 2048-bit RSA.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: A market-implied timeline anchor: roughly even odds of meaningful quantum advantage by 2035, useful for pacing quantum sector exposure.
insights.quote: Kalshi, as of May 2026, has an implied probability of 36% before 2030 and 52% before 2035, using the ability to simulate FeMoco and CYP450 or crack 2048-bit RSA encryption as quantum advantage benchmarks.
insights.locator: As a proxy for the view on the general consensus

insights.claim: Rigorous end-to-end studies of derivative pricing put credible quantum thresholds around 4,700 to 8,000 logical qubits with demanding speed requirements, contradicting the popular view that finance should be easier than chemistry.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Discount near-term quantum-finance hype; derivative pricing is more demanding than chemistry in qubit terms, so finance is unlikely to be the first commercial beachhead.
insights.quote: Rigorous end-to-end studies of derivative pricing have put credible thresholds around 4,700 to 8,000 logical qubits, along with demanding speed requirements.
insights.locator: Financial use cases belong in this band too

insights.claim: A 2026 Google white paper estimated that attacking the secp256k1 elliptic-curve used in major blockchains could be done with around 1,200 to 1,450 logical qubits and fewer than 500,000 physical superconducting qubits, making ECC plausibly within reach of machines consistent with IBM, IonQ, and Diraq's public 2030-2033 roadmaps.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Blockchain/ECC signature systems may be breakable by ~2030-2033 roadmap machines, sooner than RSA-3072 assumptions imply; a tail risk for crypto-asset security and a catalyst for post-quantum migration.
insights.quote: ECC is plausibly within reach of a 500,000 to 1,000,000-qubit machine consistent with the public 2030 to 2033 roadmaps of the likes of IBM, IonQ, and Diraq.

themes.theme: Band Three: headline tasks demanding very large machines (millions of physical qubits)

insights.claim: RSA-2048 factoring estimates collapsed roughly twenty-fold through software alone, from ~20 million noisy physical qubits in eight hours (2021) to fewer than one million noisy qubits in under a week (2025).
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Algorithmic progress, not hardware, is the dominant driver pulling 'harvest-now-decrypt-later' risk forward; accelerate post-quantum cryptography migration timelines.
insights.quote: That is a roughly twenty-fold collapse in six years, achieved through software improvements alone.

insights.claim: A February 2026 paper introducing an error-correcting architecture based on quantum LDPC codes pushed the RSA-2048 requirement down another order of magnitude to fewer than 100,000 physical qubits, under identical hardware assumptions to the prior estimate.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: qLDPC codes are a step-change in error-correction efficiency; the firms and teams advancing qLDPC may compress fault-tolerant timelines materially.
insights.quote: a February 2026 paper introducing a new error-correcting architecture based on quantum LDPC codes (a form of quantum error-correcting codes) pushed that figure down by another order of magnitude to fewer than 100,000 physical qubits

insights.claim: For universal gate-based machines, the strongest rigorous study on hard optimisation found that crossover with classical methods could require roughly 8.9 million to 73.9 million physical qubits, and most optimisation claims rely on QAOA (not yet proven for generic advantage) or non-universal quantum annealing.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Be most skeptical of optimisation marketing claims; generic quantum optimisation advantage remains a multi-million-qubit, algorithmically unproven prospect.
insights.quote: crossover with classical methods could require roughly anywhere between the wide range of 8.9 million to 73.9 million physical qubits.
insights.locator: Optimisation is the most commercially marketed

insights.claim: D-Wave already offers systems with more than 4,400 physical qubits for specialised annealing workflows, but those are not comparable to fault-tolerant logical qubits because many physical annealer qubits are tied together to represent one problem variable.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Do not compare D-Wave's 4,400+ annealer qubits to gate-based logical-qubit counts; specialised annealing value exists today but is a different category from broad fault-tolerant advantage.
insights.quote: D-Wave already offers systems with more than 4,400 physical qubits for specialised workflows, but those qubits are not directly comparable to logical qubits in a fault-tolerant gate-based computer
insights.locator: Quantum annealers tell a different story

themes.theme: Investment implication: algorithmic compression outpaces hardware

insights.claim: Across all three bands, quantum resource estimates are collapsing rapidly through algorithmic improvement, consistently outpacing hardware progress and trending toward fewer qubits and shorter runtimes for the same outcome.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: Model quantum timelines with an aggressive algorithmic-improvement discount curve, not just hardware roadmaps; software/algorithm teams may be undervalued relative to hardware.
insights.quote: The trend that runs across each band is that resource estimates are collapsing rapidly through algorithmic improvement, outpacing even hardware progress
insights.locator: What This Means for Capital and Conviction

insights.claim: The right diligence question is not how many qubits a company has but how many reliable qubits its target application actually needs and how credibly its roadmap closes that gap, interrogating logical-qubit translation, modality assumptions, and algorithmic compression.
insights.speaker: Quantum Computing Report
insights.so_what: A concrete diligence checklist for screening quantum investments: application-specific reliable-qubit need vs. roadmap, not headline raw-qubit count.
insights.quote: The right question is rarely how many qubits a company has. It is how many reliable qubits its target application actually needs, and how credibly its roadmap closes the gap.
insights.locator: Different applications will mature at very different rates
